decomposition.title: RFP - Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
decomposition.industry: Manufacturing, enterprise solutions, hospitality, digital innovation, IT infrastructure hosting
decomposition.estimated_deal_size_usd: No explicit budget or contract value is stated. Inferred potential downstream opportunity: approximately $15M-$50M over 3 years, based on a large Wisconsin data center exit evaluation covering enterprise applications, SAP, VMware/VxRail, physical servers, Windows/Linux, multiple databases, backup/DR, network connectivity, monitoring/logging/security tooling, and possible hosting/DCaaS/hybrid services. The Phase 0 evaluation effort itself is likely materially smaller than the downstream hosting transformation.
decomposition.contract_duration: Not explicitly stated in the provided document. The RFP is a Phase 0 evaluation and does not commit to a specific execution approach; any downstream hosting/DCaaS/hybrid arrangement is inferred to likely be a multi-year contract, approximately 3-5 years, based on enterprise hosting scope and typical infrastructure sourcing models.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed solution covering target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles.
Vendors must cover the landing zone deployment model and application deployment model.
Vendors must cover environment segregation for Prod / Test / Dev.
Vendors must cover identity integration with enterprise identity providers.
Vendors must cover hybrid connectivity approach.
Vendors must cover region strategy and data residency approach.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data residency is listed inside scope bullets without specific jurisdictions; bidders could miss the need to explicitly address residency strategy.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Specific residency jurisdictions are not stated; current data center is Wisconsin-based and Kohler operates across multiple regions.

requirements.text: Vendors must cover disaster recovery, high availability, and scalability patterns.
Architecture diagrams, reference designs, and supporting documentation are strongly encouraged.
Vendors must respond for relevant scenarios, Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid, where applicable, and clearly state assumptions.
The in-scope services include enterprise applications currently hosted in the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center.
The in-scope services include commercial off-the-shelf and internally developed applications.
The in-scope services include Windows and Linux virtual machines.
The in-scope services include databases including SQL Server, Oracle, SAP HANA, MySQL, PostgreSQL, and other supported platforms.
The in-scope services include middleware and integration platforms.
The in-scope services include file storage systems and shared infrastructure services.
The in-scope services include backup and disaster recovery solutions.
The in-scope services include identity and access management integrations.
The in-scope services include network connectivity services across enterprise and manufacturing environments.
The in-scope services include monitoring, logging, and security tooling.
The in-scope services include selected OT-adjacent workloads, limited to read-only or controlled integration scenarios.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: OT-adjacent scope is narrow and limited; over-proposing uncontrolled OT scope may create misalignment.

requirements.text: Vendors are to use the detailed inventory of in-scope applications and infrastructure attached in the RFP package online; the inventory spreadsheet provides counts and sizing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The inventory spreadsheet is external to the main RFP and drives sizing and pricing; missing it would undermine proposal compliance.

requirements.text: Detailed migration execution and implementation services, including actual workload movement, cutover planning, and execution activities, are out of scope.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Execution is explicitly out of scope for this Phase 0 RFP despite later pricing references to migration/implementation costs.

requirements.text: Application refactoring, remediation, or modernization activities, including code changes, re-platforming, and re-architecting, are out of scope.
Detailed low-level design and configuration-level architecture beyond high-level solution design and reference architecture are out of scope.
Operational transition and steady-state managed services delivery commitments, including full-scale IT operations and support models, are out of scope.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Steady-state managed services delivery commitments are out of scope for Phase 0 even though pricing asks for any consulting, support, or managed services costs.

requirements.text: Execution of security controls, compliance certification, or regulatory audits is out of scope; vendors are only required to demonstrate capability and readiness.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Vendors must demonstrate capability/readiness, not perform certification or audits in this phase.

requirements.text: Tool implementation, automation deployment, or integration development is out of scope unless required to illustrate proposed solution capability.
End-user training, change management execution, and organizational transformation activities are out of scope.
Data cleansing, data transformation, or archival activities beyond indicative approaches are out of scope.
Detailed testing execution and validation activities, including UAT, SIT, or performance testing, are out of scope; only approach and methodology are expected.
Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards, and evidence of certifications is required.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is buried in the Compliance & Regulatory Requirements section and the specific certifications are not named, creating a high-risk compliance evidence gap.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry/regional standards are not specified in the provided text.
requirements.cert_required: Evidence of certifications required for applicable industry/regional standards.

requirements.text: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This is a provider-type-specific mandatory attestation requirement and points to the Vendor Response Template, which is outside the main document.
requirements.cert_required: Compliance attestations as per industry standards for DCaaS providers.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to review, validate, and clearly state any deviations or additional assumptions in their proposal.
Vendors are expected to propose Cloud, DCaaS, and/or Hybrid hosting solutions aligned to Kohler’s stated objectives and constraints.
Vendors are expected to base their response on the provided inventory dataset.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The provided inventory dataset is a required baseline, but it is not included in the document text.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to highlight any data gaps or assumptions made due to incomplete information.
Vendors are expected to use the provided inventory as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Sizing and indicative pricing must tie to an external inventory baseline not included in the text.

requirements.text: Where data gaps exist, vendors must clearly state assumptions used for sizing and costing.
Responses must be complete, clearly structured, and aligned to each requirement.
Vendors must clearly state all assumptions, dependencies, and constraints associated with their response.
Any optional services or additional capabilities not covered under the scope must be explicitly identified separately.
Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Proposal deadline and communication route requirements are in the response instructions, not the summary; late or misdirected submissions may never be evaluated.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple ‘Yes/No’ answers will not be considered sufficient.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: A superficially completed template with Yes/No responses is expressly insufficient and may fail before substantive evaluation.

requirements.text: Vendors must provide high-level indicative solution designs and supporting explanations where relevant.
All clarification queries must be submitted within the defined timeline and use the template for Q&A provided to the contact provided.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Clarification process requires a specific timeline and Q&A template; failure to use it may waive ability to clarify hidden requirements.

requirements.text: Vendors are required to submit their responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of this RFP to ensure consistency and comparability across all submissions.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Mandatory response template is in a short templates section and can be missed; non-template responses may be non-comparable.

requirements.text: Pricing must be transparent, structured, and clearly itemized, aligned to the requirements of this RFP and the inventory baseline provided.
Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup including foundational setup costs such as landing zone, security, governance, and compliance configurations.
Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup including application and infrastructure hosting costs for compute, storage, networking, and platform services.
Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup including migration / implementation costs, if applicable, including transition or onboarding activities.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Migration execution is out of scope, but pricing asks for migration/implementation costs if applicable.

requirements.text: Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup including any consulting, support, or managed services costs.
Vendors must provide separate cost views for SAP and non-SAP workloads, ensuring clear visibility into cost drivers across application categories.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Separate SAP/non-SAP pricing is a specific mandatory cost view and can materially affect comparability.

requirements.text: All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD).
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Currency requirement is a simple but absolute commercial formatting rule; non-USD pricing may be non-compliant.

requirements.text: Proposals should include cost increase protections and efficiency gains that lead to a reduction or no more than a reasonable increase over time.
Payment terms are an important part of the proposal; anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, and proposals should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The document states payment terms below net 90 will not be competitive; this is not phrased as automatic rejection but is a major commercial risk.

requirements.text: Vendors will be evaluated on target state architecture, security, and governance capabilities.
Vendors will be evaluated on migration approach, risk considerations, and transition strategy.
Vendors will be evaluated on networking and connectivity capabilities.
Vendors will be evaluated on availability, resilience, and disaster recovery approach.
Vendors will be evaluated on cost and commercial transparency.
Vendors will be evaluated on operating model and support capabilities.
Vendors will be evaluated on tools, ecosystem, and platform maturity.
Vendors will be evaluated on alignment to expected deliverables.
The evaluation will be based on a weighted scoring model, with indicative weightages defined as part of the Evaluation Template (Excel) provided with this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Detailed evaluation weightages are not in the main RFP and are only in the Excel template.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to provide a high-level overview of their proposed program governance model aligned to the solution being proposed.
The governance overview should include an indicative governance structure and engagement model.
The governance overview should include reporting and review mechanisms such as periodic status updates and performance tracking.
The governance overview should include an escalation framework for issue resolution.
The governance overview should include key roles and responsibilities at a high level.
Vendors must ensure that all information received from Kohler is used solely for the purpose of responding to this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Confidentiality obligations appear in Appendix 8.1 and can be missed; they restrict how partners/subcontractors can use RFP information.

requirements.text: Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This appendix clause can block sharing with third-party partners, subcontractors, hyperscalers, or advisors unless prior written consent is obtained.

requirements.text: All responses submitted as part of this RFP will be treated as confidential and used strictly for evaluation purposes.

Vendors are expected to ensure that their proposed solutions are capable of complying with applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: General compliance capability is restated in the appendix but specific requirements will be finalized later.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Relevant obligations are described only generally as applicable to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.

requirements.text: Kohler reserves the right to modify, amend, or withdraw this RFP at any stage of the process; request additional information or clarification from vendors; and accept or reject any proposal without obligation or liability.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Kohler retains broad process rights that affect bid certainty.

requirements.text: Detailed legal terms, contractual conditions, compliance requirements, and service-level agreements will be defined and agreed upon during subsequent phases following vendor selection.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Detailed contractual terms, compliance requirements, and SLAs are deferred to later phases, creating downstream non-price risk.

ambiguities: The Evaluation Template (Excel) is repeatedly referenced as containing detailed requirements, scoring, weightages, sub-categories, and DCaaS compliance information, but it is not included in the provided document; additional eliminatory requirements may exist there.

The baseline inventory dataset/spreadsheet is referenced as comprehensive and as the basis for sizing and pricing, but it is not included in the provided document; exact workload counts and deal sizing cannot be verified from this text.
No explicit budget, estimated contract value, current spend, contract term, renewal options, or implementation duration are stated in the provided document.

Applicable industry/regional standards, regulatory requirements, data protection obligations, and required certifications are not enumerated; vendors must provide evidence of certifications, but the specific certifications are unspecified.
The proposal submission channel is described as the designated communication channel with contacts listed, but no portal, email subject line, file format, or time zone for the deadline is specified.

The RFP says migration execution is out of scope for this Phase 0 evaluation, but pricing guidance requests migration/implementation costs if applicable; bidders should clarify expected pricing assumptions.
Payment terms below net 90 are stated as not competitive, but the document does not explicitly say they cause automatic rejection.
hard_disqualifiers: [PAGE 7] Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards, and evidence of certifications is required; failure to provide certification evidence risks non-compliance before evaluation.
[PAGE 7] DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards; missing attestations are a buried compliance submission risk.
[PAGE 8] Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel; late or misrouted submissions risk not being accepted.
[PAGE 8] Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple ‘Yes/No’ answers will not be considered sufficient.
[PAGE 9] Vendors are required to submit responses using the Vendor Response template provided as part of the RFP; not using the template risks non-comparable/non-compliant submission.
[PAGE 9] All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD); non-USD pricing is a mandatory commercial non-compliance risk.
[PAGE 11] Vendors must use all Kohler information solely for the purpose of responding to the RFP; other use would breach mandatory confidentiality obligations.

[PAGE 11] Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share Kohler information with third parties without prior written consent; using subcontractors or partners without consent creates a hidden legal disqualification risk.
compliance_red_flags: Evidence of certifications is required for hosting workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards, but the required certifications are not specified in the main document. See [PAGE 7].
DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards, with more detail in the Vendor Response Template that was not provided. See [PAGE 7].
Region strategy and data residency approach must be addressed, but no specific data residency jurisdictions, prohibited processing locations, or log-file requirements are stated. See [PAGE 6].
All information shared as part of the RFP is confidential and cannot be disclosed, distributed, or shared with third parties without Kohler’s prior written consent. See [PAGE 11].
The detailed legal terms, contractual conditions, compliance requirements, and SLAs will be defined in later phases, creating material downstream contractual uncertainty. See [PAGE 11].

similar_deals.title: Kohler Project Polaris — Wisconsin Data Center / Hybrid Hosting Evaluation
similar_deals.industry: Manufacturing, hospitality-adjacent enterprise infrastructure hosting, global IT
similar_deals.deal_size_usd: Phase 0 pricing variants of $279,533.42 and $595,000; downstream scenarios of $24,750,000 to $30,667,392 over approximately 36 months
similar_deals.outcome: Unknown / past proposal content only; no win claim supported by the provided intelligence
similar_deals.why_relevant: This is effectively the same pursuit profile: Kohler, Wisconsin data center exit evaluation, public cloud/DCaaS/hybrid options, SAP, VMware/VxRail, physical servers, Windows/Linux, databases, backup/DR, networking, monitoring/logging/security, and inferred downstream hosting transformation.
similar_deals.key_lesson: The best-fit proposal pattern is a 12-week vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab / Hosting Option Control Tower with Kohler-owned outputs, workload placement scorecards, landing-zone blueprints, USD TCO, FinOps guardrails, DR/security/observability design, migration waves, rollback gates, and downstream sourcing package.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation — Germany
similar_deals.why_relevant: Closest won comparator in manufacturing transformation and mid-size commercial range. Supports win themes around rapid mobilisation, domain credibility, compliance evidence, and executable transition planning.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Won because of fastest mobilisation within two weeks and strong manufacturing sector credentials. ISO 27001 and PCI-DSS L1 certifications were relevant proof points.

similar_deals.title: SAP S/4HANA Global Rollout — Germany / USA / Mexico
similar_deals.why_relevant: Highly relevant because Kohler’s hosting evaluation includes SAP and SAP HANA risk. Supports the need for SAP-specific hosting options, dependency mapping, downtime/cutover planning, rollback, DR, and executive alignment.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Won against Accenture and Deloitte due to SAP S/4HANA RISE capability, zero-downtime methodology, global follow-the-sun delivery, a decisive BMW case study, and executive sponsor alignment.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation — UK
similar_deals.why_relevant: Very close on industry, transformation type, and deal size. Directly informs the need to harden U.S. legal entity, contracting, hosting location, certifications, subcontractor, and RFP-template compliance before bid submission.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Lost because a local entity requirement in the compliance appendix was missed. Compliance appendices can be decisive even when the technical solution is viable.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation — Netherlands
similar_deals.why_relevant: Close manufacturing IT transformation comparator in the same commercial band. Reinforces that a compliance owner and red-team review are mandatory before bidding.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Lost because a local entity requirement in the compliance appendix was missed. Repeated pattern confirms that administrative/legal compliance failures are a serious bid risk in manufacturing transformations.

similar_deals.title: Banking IT Transformation — USA
similar_deals.why_relevant: Not manufacturing, but relevant to U.S. enterprise transformation, security/compliance posture, PCI-sensitive environments, local legal entity readiness, and similar downstream deal size.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Won because the required local entity was in place and certifications such as ISO 27001 and PCI-DSS L1 supported credibility. Pre-existing relationship also helped.

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Estimated win probability is 46% with medium confidence. Evidence is strong on solution fit but incomplete on market/competitor validation. The past proposal knowledge base contains an exact/near-exact Kohler Project Polaris pattern with highly reusable content: a 12-week vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab, workload placement scorecards, Azure-first/hybrid landing-zone blueprints, IAM/security/DR/observability designs, open-book USD TCO, FinOps guardrails, migration waves, rollback gates, and downstream sourcing package. Pricing anchors of $279,533 and $595,000 for Phase 0 and $24.75M-$30.67M for downstream modernization are directly relevant. The deal corpus also supports the strategy: DEAL-089, a $22M manufacturing transformation, was won through rapid mobilisation and domain credentials; DEAL-003 shows SAP manufacturing credibility can be decisive; DEAL-038 and DEAL-094 show proprietary IP/accelerators reduce perceived risk; DEAL-065 supports U.S. compliance and local-entity readiness. However, probability is held below 50% because no public procurement data is available to validate actual competitor win patterns, award pricing, or incumbent advantage; the Kohler-specific past proposal record does not state an outcome; and the RFP has several hard disqualification risks around certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, detailed template completion, USD pricing, submission channel, confidentiality, and subcontractor consent. Internal loss patterns also require pessimism: DEAL-056 and DEAL-100 were similar manufacturing transformation losses caused by missed local-entity/compliance appendix requirements. If certification/DCaaS evidence, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, region/data residency, SAP/database detail, and compliance governance are fixed before submission, the bid is credible; without those fixes, win probability should fall materially.
capability_gaps: Certification evidence gap: RFP page 7 requires evidence of certifications for hosting workloads under applicable industry/regional standards. Intelligence shows relevant ISO 27001 and PCI-DSS L1 credentials in DEAL-089 and DEAL-065, but the Kohler-specific proposal excerpts do not confirm the exact certification evidence package, SOC reports, cloud/DCaaS attestations, or hosting-location-specific compliance artifacts to be submitted.

DCaaS attestation gap: RFP page 7 requires DCaaS providers to provide compliance attestations. The past proposal positions cloud/DCaaS/hybrid evaluation well, but no named DCaaS facility/provider attestations, SOC reports, uptime/resilience certifications, or data-center location evidence are present in the provided intelligence.

Environment segregation gap: the Kohler-specific proposal content does not explicitly include a full Prod/Test/Dev segregation design, although related manufacturing hosting content references development/test/quality/production environments. This must be made explicit to satisfy the must-have requirement for environment segregation.

Identity integration detail gap: proposal intelligence references IAM baselining, enterprise identity, privileged access, Entra ID/SaaS integration, and governance, but does not confirm Kohler’s actual identity provider, federation model, privileged-access tooling, conditional-access policies, or integration assumptions.

Region strategy and data residency gap: the proposal includes global/multi-region and hybrid concepts, but the provided intelligence does not define a Kohler-specific region/data residency matrix for SAP, databases, hospitality/payment-related workloads, connected products, logs, backups, and disaster recovery copies.

SAP/database hosting evidence gap: the RFP scope includes SAP HANA, Oracle, SQL Server, MySQL, PostgreSQL, middleware, and integration platforms. Deal corpus shows SAP strength in DEAL-003, but the Kohler-specific excerpts need more explicit platform-by-platform hosting, licensing, HA/DR, latency, backup, and operational ownership treatment.

Subcontractor/partner consent gap: RFP page 11 restricts disclosure or sharing of Kohler information with third parties without prior written consent. If cloud hyperscalers, DCaaS partners, network providers, or tooling vendors are used in solutioning, a formal consent and information-handling process is required before involving them.
recommended_win_themes: Vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab, not a forced cloud migration: position Project Polaris as an evidence-led hosting decision across public cloud, DCaaS, retained Wisconsin Data Center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration, with Kohler owning all outputs and downstream sourcing artifacts.

Manufacturing, SAP, VMware/VxRail, and database risk reduction: lead with workload-by-workload placement scorecards, SAP dependency mapping, VMware/VxRail disposition, database hosting patterns, RTO/RPO tiering, migration waves, rollback gates, and manufacturing/hospitality continuity controls.

Rapid Phase 0 mobilisation with concrete deliverables: use the DEAL-089 lesson that fastest mobilisation helped win a manufacturing transformation. Commit to a named 12-week plan with discovery, dependency mapping, landing-zone/security design, hybrid connectivity, TCO/FinOps, migration sequencing, and executive decision package.

Compliance-first and audit-ready hosting evaluation: directly address the loss pattern from DEAL-056 and DEAL-100 by making certifications, U.S. legal entity, data residency, DCaaS attestations, subcontractor consent, security controls, and RFP template compliance visible rather than administrative afterthoughts.

Open-book economics and Kohler-controlled optionality: emphasize USD-only TCO, FinOps guardrails, cloud-vs-DCaaS-vs-hybrid scenario modeling, clear assumptions, and no lock-in to HCLTech or any one hosting provider during Phase 0.
risk_factors: No public procurement data is available, so there is no ground-truth award history to validate competitor win rates, pricing benchmarks, incumbent advantage, or likely awarded contract value. This reduces confidence and prevents increasing win probability based on market evidence.

The strongest past proposal intelligence is an exact/near-exact Kohler Project Polaris proposal record, but the provided data does not state that it was won. It proves relevance of content and pricing patterns, not client preference or win momentum.
Kohler may use Phase 0 only to inform a later competitive sourcing event. Winning the evaluation does not guarantee the downstream $15M-$50M hosting/DCaaS/hybrid transformation.

Mandatory compliance risk is material. RFP disqualifiers include certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, detailed response template completion, USD pricing, submission method/timing, and confidentiality/subcontractor restrictions.

Manufacturing transformation losses in DEAL-056 and DEAL-100 show that local/legal/compliance appendix failures can eliminate otherwise credible bids. This is a proven internal loss pattern and must lower confidence until compliance is fully locked.

Competitor landscape is not validated by procurement data. Deal corpus indicates we have won against Accenture, Deloitte, IBM, Wipro, Cognizant, DXC, Fujitsu, Capgemini, and Atos in some comparable deals, but also lost to TCS and Infosys in manufacturing transformation deals. No assumption should be made that competitors are weak here.

The RFP requires credible comparison of Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid. If the response appears biased toward Azure-first cloud migration or toward HCLTech downstream managed services, it may conflict with the evaluation nature of Phase 0.

The scope spans SAP, VMware/VxRail, physical servers, Windows/Linux, Oracle, SQL Server, SAP HANA, MySQL, PostgreSQL, middleware, file storage, backup/DR, monitoring/logging/security, and network connectivity. Under-detailing any of these could materially reduce technical score.
must_fix_before_bidding: Build a mandatory compliance matrix covering every RFP requirement and disqualifier, including template use, USD pricing, submission channel, confidentiality, certification evidence, subcontractor disclosure, DCaaS attestations, and no Yes/No-only responses.

Attach actual certification and attestation evidence: ISO 27001, SOC 1/SOC 2 as applicable, PCI-DSS where hospitality/payment environments may be affected, cloud security certifications, DCaaS facility attestations, and data-center/location compliance documentation.

Add a Kohler-specific Prod/Test/Dev/DR/shared-services segregation architecture using account/subscription separation, network segmentation, RBAC/PAM boundaries, policy-as-code, secrets isolation, logging separation, backup policies, and controlled CI/CD promotion paths.

Confirm and document U.S. legal entity, contracting model, insurance, tax/Wisconsin considerations, local support coverage, data residency commitments, and any subcontractor/partner participation before submission. This directly addresses the repeated loss pattern in DEAL-056 and DEAL-100 where local/compliance appendix issues caused losses.

Make the Phase 0 proposal explicitly vendor-neutral and outcome-owned by Kohler, with all cloud/DCaaS/hybrid assumptions clearly stated, open-book USD TCO, workload placement scorecards, and a downstream RFP package that any provider can bid on.

Prepare named references and proof points in this order: U.S. manufacturing hosting/data-center exit, SAP manufacturing transformation, VMware/VxRail modernization, global/multi-region enterprise hosting, and hospitality/PCI-sensitive environments.

Price the Phase 0 carefully as a fixed-fee advisory engagement. Internal proposal intelligence shows usable anchors of $279,533 for a 12-week decision package and $595,000 for a three-month lab; choose a defensible price based on actual requested deliverables and avoid making the downstream $24.75M-$30.67M modernization estimate appear pre-decided.

competitors.likelihood_to_bid: medium-high — confidence medium. solution_options.name: Premium / Lowest Risk - Polaris Resilient Hybrid Transformation and Managed Hosting
solution_options.description: Premium / lowest-risk 36-month hybrid transformation and managed hosting model. This option provides the most complete resilience, compliance, SAP/database engineering, DCaaS attestation support, DR testing, 24x7 operations coverage and architecture governance. It is best if Kohler prioritizes certainty for SAP, manufacturing continuity, hospitality/payment-adjacent systems, connected-product platforms and global data-residency controls over lowest price.
key_components: 12-week Phase 0 Polaris Hosting Decision Lab included at the fuller historical $595K level, producing Kohler-owned workload placement scorecards, target-state diagrams, landing-zone patterns, SAP/VMware/VxRail dependency map, DR/HA blueprint, RACI, FinOps model and compliance-attestation checklist.

People cost model: 26 FTE blended team x 6240 hours over 36 months x about $85/hour = about $13.8M. Team includes delivery executive, service delivery manager, enterprise/cloud architects, SAP/platform architect, VMware/DCaaS architect, SREs, DBAs, network engineers, security analysts, FinOps lead, ITSM/process lead and 24x7 operations coverage for critical roles.

Infrastructure model uses live API rates scaled to enterprise size: 300 mixed EC2 instances using m5.xlarge at $0.192/hr, m5.2xlarge at $0.384/hr, m5.4xlarge at $0.768/hr, r5.xlarge at $0.252/hr, c5.xlarge at $0.170/hr and t3.medium at $0.0416/hr; 36 RDS instances using db.m5.xlarge PostgreSQL at $0.462/hr, db.m5.2xlarge at $0.924/hr and db.r5.xlarge at $0.578/hr; 180 TB RDS gp3 at $0.115/GB-month; 650 TB S3 at $0.023/GB-month; 300 TB Azure RA-GZRS at $0.1265/GB-month; 150 TB Azure ZRS at $0.075/GB-month; and 70 TB/month egress at $0.09/GB. Raw cloud is about $6.45M, plus about $0.95M for network, security, backup, monitoring, licensing and DCaaS/hybrid extensions = $7.40M infrastructure.

Transformation includes landing-zone build, Prod/Test/Dev segregation design, identity/federation integration, hybrid connectivity, SAP/database HA/DR engineering, dependency mapping, migration factories, testing, parallel run and rollback gates.
Governance includes architecture review board, compliance evidence management, FinOps, service reporting, ITSM integration, observability/AIOps patterns and vendor coexistence controls.
solution_options.rationale: Best fit where Kohler wants maximum resilience and the lowest execution risk. Pricing math: People $13.800M + Infrastructure $7.400M + Migration/Transformation $3.100M + Governance/Compliance $1.610M = $25.910M direct cost; Contingency $3.1075M brings total cost to $29.0175M; 20.5% gross margin $7.4825M gives total client price $36.500M. This aligns to the upper end of past Polaris downstream proposal patterns around $35.7M, but it is above the $22M-$32M competitor PTW corridor and therefore is not the recommended win-price.

People cost model: 22 FTE blended team x 6240 hours over 36 months x about $83/hour = about $11.4M. Staffing includes service delivery manager, architecture lead, cloud/DCaaS architect, SAP/database SMEs, migration leads, SRE/cloud operations, DBA, network, security, FinOps, ITSM and governance roles with 24x7 coverage for critical production services.

Infrastructure model uses live API rates scaled to enterprise size: 260 mixed EC2 instances using 120 m5.xlarge at $0.192/hr, 40 m5.2xlarge at $0.384/hr, 20 m5.4xlarge at $0.768/hr, 30 r5.xlarge at $0.252/hr, 20 c5.xlarge at $0.170/hr and 50 t3.medium at $0.0416/hr; 26 RDS PostgreSQL instances using db.m5.xlarge at $0.462/hr, db.m5.2xlarge at $0.924/hr and db.r5.xlarge at $0.578/hr; 120 TB RDS gp3 at $0.115/GB-month; 400 TB S3 at $0.023/GB-month; 200 TB Azure RA-GZRS at $0.1265/GB-month; 100 TB Azure ZRS at $0.075/GB-month; and 40 TB/month egress at $0.09/GB. Raw cloud is about $4.38M, plus about $1.52M for network, security, backup, monitoring, licensing and DCaaS/hybrid extensions = $5.90M infrastructure.

Transformation scope includes workload baselining, target-state architecture, multi-account / multi-subscription landing-zone design, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, identity integration assumptions, hybrid connectivity, region/data-residency matrix, SAP/database hosting patterns, migration-wave plan, testing, DR/HA design and rollback gates.

Governance includes ITSM operating model, vendor/Cognizant coexistence model, compliance evidence package, DCaaS attestation checklist, open-book 3-year TCO, FinOps controls, security-by-design guardrails and observability/AIOps integration.
solution_options.rationale: Recommended because it is evidence-based, competitive and executable. It is anchored to prior HCLTech Polaris downstream pricing of about $30.67M and remains within the supplied $22M-$32M price-to-win band. Pricing math: People $11.400M + Infrastructure $5.900M + Migration/Transformation $3.000M + Governance/Compliance $1.600M = $21.900M direct cost; Contingency $2.64375M, about 12.1%, brings total cost to $24.54375M; 17.5% gross margin $5.20625M gives total client price $29.750M. This should be the commercial lead position because it undercuts likely premium competitors while preserving enough delivery capacity for SAP, databases, DR, security and global hybrid operations.

solution_options.name: Lean / Lowest Cost - Essential Polaris Hybrid Hosting Transition
solution_options.description: Lean / lowest-cost 36-month model. This option preserves the must-have architecture and evaluation deliverables but narrows transformation depth, reduces dedicated SME coverage, limits modernization, uses more offshore operations leverage, and accepts higher execution risk around SAP/database complexity, DCaaS attestations, DR testing depth and parallel-run duration.
key_components: Lean Phase 0 uses the lower historical Polaris advisory benchmark of about $279K-$300K, with fewer workshops and greater reliance on Kohler-provided inventory, CMDB, cost and dependency data.

People cost model: 19 FTE blended team x 6240 hours over 36 months x about $78/hour = about $9.25M, rounded to $9.3M. Staffing covers essential delivery management, architecture, migration, SRE, DBA, network, security and ITSM functions but with less dedicated depth than Options A or B.

Infrastructure model uses live API rates scaled to a smaller / more aggressively rationalized enterprise footprint: 220 mixed EC2 instances using 100 m5.xlarge at $0.192/hr, 30 m5.2xlarge at $0.384/hr, 10 m5.4xlarge at $0.768/hr, 20 r5.xlarge at $0.252/hr, 20 c5.xlarge at $0.170/hr and 40 t3.medium at $0.0416/hr; 17 RDS PostgreSQL instances using db.m5.xlarge at $0.462/hr, db.m5.2xlarge at $0.924/hr and db.r5.xlarge at $0.578/hr; 90 TB RDS gp3 at $0.115/GB-month; 300 TB S3 at $0.023/GB-month; 150 TB Azure RA-GZRS at $0.1265/GB-month; and 25 TB/month egress at $0.09/GB. Raw cloud is about $2.97M, plus about $2.13M for essential network, security, backup, monitoring, licensing and DCaaS/hybrid extensions = $5.10M infrastructure.

Transformation includes baseline architecture, workload placement, landing-zone patterns, essential identity and connectivity design, limited migration factory, limited parallel run and standard DR/HA patterns rather than full workload-by-workload engineering.

Governance includes minimum viable ITSM, compliance checklist, monthly service reporting and basic FinOps; more advanced AIOps, detailed data-residency matrices and deep SAP/database HA validation would require change control.
solution_options.rationale: Lowest-cost compliant posture and useful as a defensive price if Kohler is highly cost constrained. It remains above the $1M enterprise floor and is aligned with the low end of past downstream Polaris pricing around $24.75M, but it carries higher risk because staffing, migration depth, DR testing and platform-specific SAP/database engineering are compressed. Pricing math: People $9.300M + Infrastructure $5.100M + Migration/Transformation $2.500M + Governance/Compliance $1.390M = $18.290M direct cost; Contingency $2.20125M brings total cost to $20.49125M; 15.5% gross margin $3.75875M gives total client price $24.250M.

pricing.pricing_structure: 36-month total program price including a fixed-fee 12-week Phase 0 vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab, downstream migration / transformation, landing-zone build, hybrid connectivity design, governance, and multi-year managed hosting operations. Phase 0 remains commercially separable and should prohibit resale/referral margin; downstream execution is assumed to be separately authorized by Kohler after the decision gate. Math for recommended Option B: People $11.400M + Infrastructure $5.900M + Migration $3.000M + Governance $1.600M + Contingency $2.64375M + Margin $5.20625M = $29.750M.

cost_drivers.category: People / managed services - 22 FTE blended 36-month team, including SDM, architects, SRE, cloud ops, DBA, network, security, FinOps and ITSM roles; 22 FTE x 6240 hours x about $83/hour
Infrastructure - enterprise-scaled AWS/Azure raw cloud plus network, security, backup, monitoring, licensing and DCaaS/hybrid extensions; live API raw-cloud component about $4.38M
Transformation / migration - Phase 0 Hosting Decision Lab plus workload dependency mapping, migration waves, landing-zone implementation, testing, cutover and rollback planning
Governance, tooling and compliance - ITSM, observability, AIOps integration, FinOps dashboards, compliance evidence package, architecture authority and reporting
Contingency - about 12.1% of pre-contingency delivery cost for discovery gaps, SAP/database complexity, parallel run and DCaaS attestation uncertainty
Margin - 17.5% gross margin, aligned with realistic IT services margin and competitor PTW math

pricing.competitive_rationale: Recommended Option B is anchored in HCLTech's most relevant Kohler Project Polaris proposal history: $279K-$595K for Phase 0 and $24.75M-$35.7M for broader 36-month transformation / managed-hosting scenarios. No public award benchmark was supplied, so market validation relies on the provided competitor price-to-win corridor of $22M-$32M over 3 years. The $29.75M recommendation is inside that corridor, close to the prior $30.67M Polaris control-tower pattern, and below premium Accenture / Deloitte-style pricing while avoiding the quality and resilience risk of a DXC-floor bid. Live AWS/Azure unit pricing was used for the infrastructure layer and scaled to an enterprise estate rather than a small pilot.

draft.executive_summary: Project Polaris is not a binary cloud-versus-data-center choice. It is a resilience, cost, architecture, security, and operating-model decision for Kohler’s Wisconsin Data Center, global plants, hospitality operations, enterprise platforms, SAP dependencies, VMware and VxRail estate, analytics environments, connected products, and customer-facing digital experiences. Kohler’s stated CTO and CDO priorities are clear: use digital and data to simplify processes, deepen engagement, drive innovation, advance AI, analytics, platforms and cybersecurity, and improve productivity and customer value through platforms and digital engineering. Our response is therefore a vendor-neutral 12-week Polaris Hosting Decision Lab, not a forced migration bid. HCLTech will compare public cloud, DCaaS, retained Wisconsin Data Center, hybrid hosting, plant and edge hosting, and SaaS integration workload by workload. Kohler will own every output: the decision model, executable architecture diagrams, SAP and VMware dependency maps, compliance-attestation checklist, DR and HA design, migration waves, rollback gates, FinOps guardrails, and three-year USD TCO scenarios. The differentiator is contractual neutrality: during Phase 0, we will not earn resale margin, referral fees, or downstream hosting or run-services revenue unless Kohler separately reopens competition. Polaris becomes a Kohler-owned hosting decision engine: evidence-led, manufacturing-continuity first, security-by-design, and commercially transparent, enabling leadership to decide with confidence while plants, SAP, hospitality operations, and digital customer experiences continue to perform.
win_themes: Vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab, not a forced cloud migration.
Manufacturing, SAP, VMware/VxRail, and database risk reduction.
Rapid Phase 0 mobilisation with concrete 12-week deliverables.

sections.section_title: Target state architecture, security and governance
sections.content: Kohler’s target state must protect operating continuity while giving leadership a defensible hosting decision. HCLTech will build a vendor-neutral reference architecture covering public cloud, DCaaS, retained Wisconsin Data Center, hybrid, plant and edge, and SaaS integration patterns. We will not begin with an assumed cloud-first answer; we will classify each workload by business criticality, latency, regulatory exposure, SAP dependency, VMware or VxRail disposition, database pattern, integration complexity, RTO/RPO tier, and commercial sensitivity. The architecture will define landing-zone patterns, identity federation, privileged access, segmentation, encryption, key management, logging, backup, policy-as-code, data residency, and shared responsibility across Kohler, incumbents, DCaaS providers, cloud providers, and SaaS vendors. Governance will include an architecture decision board, security control traceability, risk acceptance workflow, FinOps guardrails, change gates, and executive decision criteria. The output is a Kohler-owned decision model with executable diagrams and security evidence, giving Polaris a compliance-first, manufacturing-continuity foundation for future innovation.

sections.section_title: Migration approach, risk and transition strategy
sections.content: Project Polaris should reduce transition risk before any migration commitment is made. Our 12-week Hosting Decision Lab will baseline applications, infrastructure, SAP landscapes, VMware and VxRail clusters, physical servers, databases, storage, backup, monitoring, contracts, and operational ownership. We will convert that baseline into workload placement scorecards and migration waves that show prerequisites, dependency groups, cutover windows, test evidence, rollback gates, data synchronization approach, DR validation, and business impact. Manufacturing and hospitality continuity will be treated as gating criteria, not later workstream details. SAP-connected, plant-facing, customer-facing, and analytics workloads will receive explicit sequencing and regression considerations. Risks will be maintained in a living register covering latency, licensing, vendor coexistence, security controls, data movement, operational readiness, and change saturation. The result is a transition strategy Kohler can execute with HCLTech or any downstream provider, preserving optionality while making each migration decision evidence-led.

Hosting choice is constrained by network reality. HCLTech will map Kohler’s connectivity across the Wisconsin Data Center, plants, hospitality locations, enterprise users, cloud regions, SaaS platforms, suppliers, and partner ecosystems. We will evaluate hybrid connectivity options including private cloud interconnects, DCaaS cross-connects, SD-WAN patterns, internet breakout, DNS, IP address management, firewall zones, load balancing, remote access, and segmentation for operational technology-adjacent plant workloads. Each hosting option will be scored for latency, bandwidth, resilience, security inspection, routing complexity, cost, and operational support. We will also define connectivity patterns for SAP interfaces, databases, analytics and AI data flows, monitoring feeds, backup replication, and identity services. This network model supports the vendor-neutral win theme by showing the practical trade-offs between cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, and edge patterns before Kohler commits capital or operational risk.

Kohler’s hosting roadmap must improve resilience without creating avoidable disruption. HCLTech will classify workloads into availability and recovery tiers using business criticality, RTO, RPO, manufacturing impact, hospitality impact, customer experience impact, integration dependencies, and data protection obligations. For each tier, we will define HA and DR patterns across retained data center, DCaaS, public cloud, hybrid, plant edge, and SaaS options. The design will address backup immutability, replication, failover orchestration, DR test frequency, recovery runbooks, monitoring triggers, incident escalation, and evidence required for audit. SAP, database, VMware/VxRail, and plant-facing workloads will receive explicit dependency and rollback analysis. We will avoid generic resilience claims by tying each recommendation to cost, risk, operational ownership, and testability. Polaris will therefore produce a practical DR and HA design that strengthens continuity while keeping Kohler in control of the final hosting path.

The commercial model will be open-book, USD-only, and designed to prevent lock-in. For Phase 0, HCLTech will operate under a vendor-neutral model that prohibits resale margin, referral fees, or downstream hosting and run-services revenue unless Kohler separately reopens competition. We will build three-year TCO scenarios for public cloud, DCaaS, retained Wisconsin Data Center, hybrid, plant and edge, and SaaS integration options. Each scenario will separate one-time transition cost, recurring hosting cost, network cost, software and license impact, tooling, backup, DR, operational support, security controls, and exit considerations. Assumptions will be explicit, adjustable, and traceable to workload data. FinOps guardrails will cover tagging, budgets, chargeback or showback, rightsizing, reserved capacity, storage lifecycle, and consumption anomaly detection. This gives Kohler a commercially transparent decision package and protects optionality across providers, platforms, and future sourcing events.

A hosting decision only works if the operating model is clear. HCLTech will define the future-state RACI across Kohler teams, incumbent providers, cloud providers, DCaaS providers, SaaS vendors, security operations, network operations, service desk, application owners, and plant or hospitality stakeholders. The model will cover incident, problem, change, release, capacity, configuration, asset, patch, vulnerability, backup, DR, access, service reporting, and continual improvement processes. We will identify where Kohler should retain control and where managed services or provider responsibilities can be safely delegated. AIOps and observability will be positioned as an operating layer for event correlation, noise reduction, service health, and reliability insights, consistent with Kohler’s AI, platforms, cybersecurity, and productivity priorities. The forward path is a hybrid support model that reduces ambiguity, accelerates decisions, and keeps critical manufacturing and customer experiences stable.

The Hosting Decision Lab will assess the maturity of the toolchain required to operate any selected model. HCLTech will evaluate current and target capabilities for discovery, CMDB quality, dependency mapping, infrastructure as code, policy automation, monitoring, logging, SIEM integration, AIOps, backup, DR orchestration, vulnerability management, cost management, license tracking, and service reporting. We will compare how these tools support public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, VMware and VxRail, SAP, databases, plant and edge environments, and SaaS integration. The assessment will also identify ecosystem constraints, including provider certifications, DCaaS attestations, subcontractor consent requirements, data residency, and interoperability with incumbent operations. Recommendations will prioritize tools that Kohler can own, reuse, or source competitively. This reinforces the no-lock-in theme by making platform maturity visible before execution commitments are made.

HCLTech will mobilize Polaris as a 12-week decision factory with concrete deliverables. Weeks 1-2 will confirm scope, stakeholders, access, data sources, decision criteria, and governance. Weeks 3-4 will inventory applications, infrastructure, SAP, VMware/VxRail, databases, network, backup, security controls, monitoring, contracts, and costs. Weeks 5-6 will map dependencies and establish RTO/RPO, performance, compliance, and operational baselines. Weeks 7-8 will compare public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant and edge, and SaaS options through workload placement scorecards. Weeks 9-10 will produce target architecture, landing-zone patterns, hybrid connectivity, security controls, DR tiers, observability, and FinOps guardrails. Weeks 11-12 will finalize migration waves, rollback gates, RACI, risk register, compliance-attestation checklist, three-year USD TCO, and executive decision package. The deliverables will be Kohler-owned and executable, turning Polaris into an evidence-led roadmap rather than a supplier sales funnel.

critical_issues: The competitor analysis is largely unsupported by the provided raw data. The raw financial source explicitly says Accenture financial data is unavailable and TCS financial data is unavailable, yet the agent cites detailed Accenture and TCS revenue, margin, employee-count, and pricing-floor calculations.

Most competitor financial claims for HCLTech, Kyndryl, Deloitte, TCS, and DXC are not present in the supplied SEC/financial data. They may be true externally, but they are unverified against the provided sources.

The supplied procurement data contains only unrelated UK and US public contract examples and does not validate Kohler-specific, Wisconsin-specific, or competitor-specific win patterns, incumbent advantage, or award pricing.

The agent cites many sources not included in the raw data, including earnings releases, fact sheets, job postings, LCA notices, USAspending orders, ExecutiveBiz articles, company web pages, and competitor web intelligence. These claims should not be treated as verified.

RFP page-specific requirements are unsupported. The raw data does not include RFP pages 7-11 or direct requirements for certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, detailed template completion, submission channel, confidentiality clauses, or subcontractor consent.

The internal deal corpus references DEAL-003, DEAL-038, DEAL-056, DEAL-065, DEAL-089, DEAL-094, and DEAL-100 are not present in the provided knowledge-base data and should be removed unless separately sourced.

The recommended price of $29,750,000 is plausible only for an optional downstream modernization scope. If the current bid is for Phase 0 / the 12-week Hosting Decision Lab, the verified price anchors are $279,533.42 and $595,000, so $29.75M would be materially inconsistent with the supplied Kohler pricing data.

The killer differentiator is partly supported by the Kohler knowledge-base language around vendor neutrality, no-lock-in, no-pull-through, and Kohler-owned outputs, but the claim that named competitors cannot credibly match it is unsupported by the provided data.
removed_claims: Remove or separately source all competitor revenue, margin, employee-count, revenue-per-employee, and pricing-floor calculations for Accenture, HCLTech, Kyndryl, Deloitte, TCS, and DXC.

Remove the Accenture Q3 FY26 operating margin, revenue, employee count, SAP job postings, Wisconsin LCA notice, and Wisconsin capability claims; these are not in the raw data and Accenture financial data is explicitly unavailable in the supplied financial source.
Remove the HCLTech FY26 revenue, employee count, EBIT margin, U.S. presence, careers-review, and Wisconsin hiring/contract claims; these are not in the raw data.
Remove the Kyndryl FY2026 revenue, adjusted EBITDA margin, Kyndryl Bridge, SAP/program-management job posting, and large-contract claims; these are not in the raw data.
Remove the Deloitte FY2025 revenue, employee count, Milwaukee office/job posting claims, Department of State TIOCA order, and NAVFAC $278.5M IDIQ claims; these are not in the raw data.

Remove the TCS Q1 FY27 revenue, employee count, operating margin, iBegin/careers, North America deal momentum, and Wisconsin-specific claims; TCS financial data is explicitly unavailable in the supplied financial source.
Remove the DXC Q4 FY2026 revenue, employee count, adjusted EBIT margin, GAAP EBIT margin, Amazon Quick announcement, VMware/SAP capability, and job-posting claims; these are not in the raw data.
Remove the internal deal references and outcome claims for DEAL-003, DEAL-038, DEAL-056, DEAL-065, DEAL-089, DEAL-094, and DEAL-100 unless the missing deal-corpus evidence is supplied.
Remove RFP page 7 and page 11 citations and all hard disqualification claims tied to those pages unless the actual RFP text is provided.
Remove the claim that Source 2 has no public procurement data. The supplied source does contain procurement data, though it does not contain relevant Kohler/Wisconsin/competitor award evidence.
Remove the claim that competitors cannot credibly match the vendor-neutral Phase 0 unless competitor evidence is provided.
Remove certification-specific claims such as ISO 27001 and PCI-DSS L1 credentials from DEAL-089/DEAL-065 because those deal records are not in the supplied data.
warnings: Verified Kohler knowledge-base support is strong for a 12-week/vendor-neutral Hosting Decision Lab, workload placement scorecards, target-state architecture, IAM/security/DR/observability design themes, open-book USD TCO, FinOps guardrails, migration waves, rollback gates, and a downstream sourcing package.

Verified pricing anchors are $279,533.42 and $595,000 for Phase 0 and $24.75M, $27.804672M, and $30.667392M for downstream/alternative modernization scenarios. The raw data also supports an inferred downstream opportunity of $15M-$50M+ over a multi-year term.

Pricing confidence should be reduced because there is no cloud pricing data, no Kohler budget, and no relevant public procurement award pricing in the supplied raw data. The $29.75M price is within the downstream knowledge-base range but is not independently market-validated.

Win probability should be reduced from 0.46 to 0.38 because the strongest evidence is internal/past-proposal fit, while competitor validation, procurement outcomes, incumbent data, and several cited risk factors are unsupported.
Capability gaps can be retained only as proposal-quality cautions if rewritten without unsupported RFP page citations and missing deal-corpus references.

pursuit_plan.strategy: Pursue this as a strategic advisory-led infrastructure decision engagement that positions us as the most objective partner for Kohler's Wisconsin data center exit evaluation. The response should provide a balanced Cloud/DCaaS/Hybrid decision framework, strong manufacturing-aware risk controls, certification evidence, and transparent economics without prematurely forcing a migration or managed services commitment. Use the Phase 0 work to earn trust and shape the larger downstream hosting transformation.
pursuit_plan.win_strategy: Win by being the most credible, neutral, and commercially transparent advisor for Kohler's hosting decision: prove we can compare Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid options rigorously, de-risk SAP/VMware/manufacturing workloads, and produce an executive-ready roadmap and TCO model that Kohler can trust for the larger transformation.
pursuit_plan.pricing_approach: Use a hybrid pricing approach: competitively priced fixed-fee or not-to-exceed pricing for the Phase 0 evaluation, combined with value-based scenario modeling for the downstream Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid options. The Phase 0 price should be transparent by deliverable and workstream, in USD, with assumptions tied to the missing inventory; downstream costs should be shown as directional TCO ranges with clear unit drivers, exclusions, migration/implementation options, and sensitivity analysis. Avoid pure cost-plus pricing because Kohler explicitly values commercial transparency and decision support, but also avoid aggressive fixed downstream commitments until the inventory and service levels are validated.
key_risks: The RFP is only a Phase 0 evaluation, but the downstream opportunity is much larger; over-selling migration or managed services could make the response look misaligned with stated scope.

The missing Excel evaluation template and inventory spreadsheet create major sizing, scoring, compliance, and pricing uncertainty; clarification is essential before final solution and commercial submission.
Certification requirements are mandatory but not enumerated, creating a hidden disqualification risk if compliance evidence is incomplete or not mapped clearly to cloud/DCaaS/hybrid scenarios.
Kohler may have an incumbent data center, infrastructure, SAP, or managed services provider with better knowledge of the Wisconsin environment and workload inventory.

Because evaluation weights are not provided, the proposal must balance technical architecture, transition risk, commercial transparency, and operating model instead of optimizing for one known scoring category.
OT-adjacent workloads and manufacturing connectivity introduce security, segmentation, latency, and operational-continuity risks that generic cloud proposals may underestimate.

Confidentiality rules restrict sharing Kohler information with third parties; any hyperscaler, data center partner, subcontractor, or pricing partner involvement must be approved or carefully firewalled.
Payment terms below net 90 are described as not competitive, so standard commercial terms could weaken the proposal even if not formally disqualifying.

gaps: Competitor intelligence appears materially incomplete from the provided output: only Accenture is visible, while Cognizant is a critical likely incumbent/competitor given the client intel says Kohler renewed Cognizant for AI, cloud, data centers and cybersecurity in Jan 2026.

Accenture pricing rationale is directionally plausible but weakly grounded: using corporate operating margin and revenue-per-employee does not directly support a $35M-$50M downstream prediction or Phase 0 pricing expectations.

Need clearer distinction between the Phase 0 evaluation engagement price and downstream hosting/transformation opportunity. Current intel references both, but competitor pricing mostly targets downstream transformation.
Client intel is acceptable and specific, but several signals are from broad public sources and should be treated as directional rather than definitive buyer intent.

Win intel is strong because it includes a near-identical prior proposal pattern and concrete pricing variants, but the most relevant comparator has unknown outcome, so win probability should not over-index on it.
strengths: Client intelligence includes concrete public signals: Cognizant renewal, CDO appointment, observability/AIOps, cybersecurity, cloud/data-center modernization, India Technical Center, and connected-product ecosystem.

Unstated needs are mostly actionable and tied to the RFP: hosting decision framework, hybrid operating model, observability, security-by-design, FinOps, data/AI-ready landing zones, and transition governance.
Win intel includes highly relevant internal comparator data for the same/similar Polaris hosting evaluation, including Phase 0 pricing variants and downstream scenarios.

Past-deal lessons are useful and specific: fast mobilisation, manufacturing credibility, SAP/HANA risk, zero-downtime methods, compliance evidence, local/legal entity readiness, and template compliance.
Disqualifiers are clearly identified and should help avoid non-compliance in certifications, attestations, USD pricing, template use, submission channel, confidentiality, and subcontractor consent.

agentic_decisions.reasoning: Pursue this as a strategic advisory-led infrastructure decision engagement that positions us as the most objective partner for Kohler's Wisconsin data center exit evaluation. The response should provide a balanced Cloud/DCaaS/Hybrid decision framework, strong manufacturing-aware risk controls, certification evidence, and transparent economics without prematurely forcing a migration or managed services commitment. Use the Phase 0 work to earn trust and shape the larger downstream hosting transformation.

The current Phase 2 outputs are good enough to proceed for proposal strategy, but competitor intelligence should be deepened before final pricing and competitive positioning. Client intelligence contains real, specific signals around Kohler’s Cognizant partnership, CDO appointment, cybersecurity, observability, cloud/data-center modernization, and smart-product direction. Win intel is also strong because it includes a near-identical Polaris pursuit pattern and useful lessons from manufacturing/SAP transformation deals. The main weakness is competitor coverage: Cognizant is likely the most important competitor/incumbent and is not visible in the provided competitor output, while Accenture pricing is based on indirect financial assumptions rather than comparable pursuit or rate-card evidence. This does not block proposal drafting, but it could materially affect pricing and win themes if left unresolved.
